symbols: figure bound to tree — martyrdom and willing submission to suffering for faith
bare torso — vulnerability and the exposed human body as site of sacrifice
loincloth — the last remnant of dignity preserved amid persecution
flowering landscape — beauty and life persisting despite the violence of martyrdom
tree — both the instrument of binding and a symbol of life, recalling the cross as tree
description: A nearly nude male figure stands bound to a tree trunk, his head tilted to one side with a downcast expression. He wears only a white cloth around his waist. The figure's skin is rendered in soft, pale tones against an ethereal, dreamlike background of pastel blues, pinks, and golden light. Flowers and foliage bloom around the base. The landscape behind is softly blurred with delicate colors. The overall treatment is poetic and contemplative rather than graphic, emphasizing the beauty and pathos of the figure rather than the violence of the martyrdom.
theological_description: Redon's ethereal treatment of Saint Sebastian transforms the traditional martyrdom narrative into a meditation on the beauty of sacrificial faith. Sebastian, a Roman soldier who professed Christianity under Emperor Diocletian, was sentenced to death by arrows — though tradition holds he survived the first execution, only to be beaten to death when he confronted the emperor again. His story embodies the teaching of Jesus: 'Do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul' (Matthew 10:28). The dreamlike, pastel treatment suggests that the martyr already inhabits a realm beyond physical suffering — his body is present but his spirit is elsewhere, perhaps already glimpsing the 'crown of life' promised to those who endure (Revelation 2:10). The flowering landscape surrounding the scene of suffering recalls the Christian conviction that martyrdom is not a defeat but a seed — 'the blood of the martyrs is the seed of the Church' (Tertullian). Sebastian's bound posture against the tree creates an unmistakable parallel to Christ on the cross, fulfilling the apostolic teaching that believers are called to share in Christ's sufferings (Philippians 3:10).

scripture_refs.reason: Do not fear those who kill the body — the fearlessness that motivated Sebastian's witness
Be faithful unto death and I will give you the crown of life — the promise sustaining the martyr
Sharing in Christ's sufferings — the martyr's conformity to the pattern of the cross
Present sufferings are not worth comparing with the glory to be revealed — the ethereal beauty suggesting transcendence
